svg.desc: This was the glyph from the Segoe UI Emoji font depicting code point U+1F40D (“SNAKE”), prior to the “Fluent” redesign that more or less eradicated the original work from the world over night. Then original metro era font was the first emoji typeface ever made that demonstrated responsible, competent craftsmanship. Its designers recognized that emoji are fundamentally textual in nature. They brought a clear grasp of typographic principles to bear on the project, prioritizing legibility and distinguishability at any sizes and set on any background. The glyphs actually felt like glyphs. They found many inventive, clever, and surprising solutions to (typically entirely ignored) problems that emoji had presented for many readers up to that time. Scripts in many typefaces could be set alongside it without even inducing the queasy dissonance characteristic of emoji embedded in “normal” text in other faces. There was a possibility of typographic harmony in their presence. Many of their solutions were strangely subtle — coy, even — and this was one of the most charming aspects of the type. It wasn’t just mindfully crafted, though; it had genuine artistic merit, evidently the work of real type designers who had a fantastic and playful grasp of metalanguages of shape and gesture and caricature and instananeous distinction in the eye. It wasn’t perfect — just the first of its kind, an actual invention. To date, it was also the last of its kind. It’s been gone for years, now, and Microsoft has ignored those who requested at least having the option of using it again because of low visual acuity. But for this single face, the state of emoji design has been abysmal across the board without fail and standards are far too low. Metro era Segoe UI Emoji demonstrate that there need not be anything staid or coldly medical about works that take accessibility seriously enough to wonder if the status quo is actually acceptable. Indeed, it is a natural partner to craftsmanship — perhaps even its very essence. Let it be known: no other font’s even come close to what had been achieved there seemingly with no recognition. Nothing remotely comparable to its quality preceded it nor has followed it, to date. That’s precisely what renders such achievements invisible, though; a message from the future is not often acknowledged or answered immediately even where a bar-raising challenge to prevailing standards and a way of thinking oughta be clear cause rather few are calibrated to see what’s unprecedent as it is. Anyway I was always particularly partial to this glyph and thought it oughta live again.
